tossups.question: In a 20th-century novel, a group of schoolgirls are assigned to write a letter from Sophocles to this writer from the underworld; that novel depicts two performances of this author’s plays starring the actress La Berma. The article “New Criticism or New Fraud?” attacked a psychoanalysis-inflected study of this author by Roland Barthes (“bart”). In a play by this author, the Roman Emperor Titus is forced to renounce the Queen of (*) Judaea (“joo-DEE-uh”). Théophile Gautier (“tay-oh-FEEL goat-YAY”) claimed that this writer’s only “tolerable” verse was the opening line “the daughter of Minos and Pasiphaë” (“puh-SIF-ee-ee”). In a play by this author of Berenice, which features his characteristic rhyming alexandrines, the maid Oenone (“ee-NOH-nee”) convinces the title character to declare her love for her stepson Hippolytus (“hip-AH-lit-us”). For 10 points, name this French neoclassical playwright of Phèdre (“FED-ruh”).

For 10 points, name this French neoclassical playwright of Phedre ("FED-ruh").

tossups.question: Plays by this author and an alphabetically-prior contemporary were popularised in Penguin Classics translations by Cambridge Five spy John Cairncross. Alan Hollinghurst's best-known translations are of this non-Greek author's plays. The title character deliriously demands another character's sword and is described by the allegedly untranslatable phrase "the daughter of Minos and of Pasiphae" in a play by this author, who in another play wrote about Orestes and Pyrrhus competing for Hermione. A chariot is attacked by a horned sea monster at the end of a play by this author, which uses characteristic rhymed alexandrines to depict the title character's incestuous love for Hippolyte. For 10 points, name this French playwright of Berenice and Phèdre (FED-ruh).

tossups.question: The decline of this author’s social class is compared to the way that his characters reject the world in which they are compelled to live in Lucien Goldmann’s study of this author, The Hidden God. The essay “New Criticism or New Fraud?” attacked a nontraditional book about this author, prompting a response in Criticism and Truth. The furor surrounding Raymond Picard’s polemic against a book “on” this author by Roland Barthes is credited with legitimizing theory as a discipline. Theophile Gautier claimed that this author’s finest line was the untranslatable phrase “the daughter of Minos and Pasiphaë.” This author was commissioned to write religious pieces for a girl’s boarding school by Madame de Maintenon, resulting in his work Esther. Ted Hughes spent his last months translating the rhymed alexandrine verse of this author’s play in which the appearance of a half-bull, half-snake monster causes Hippolyte to be dragged to his death. For 10 points, name this French tragedian who wrote Phedre.

For 10 points, name this French tragedian who wrote Phedre.

tossups.question: The only comedic play by this author culminates when a judge rules against a dog for eating a chicken, but pardons it after pleas from its puppies. This author of The Litigants wrote another play in which Junia becomes a priestess and the emperor poisons the title character at a feast. In one of this playwright’s works, Hermione asks her cousin to kill (*) Pyrrhus, who loves the title character. This author wrote a play in which the nurse Oenone drowns herself before her former mistress can be questioned by the newly returned King of Athens. In that play by this user of Alexandrine verse, the wife of Theseus accuses her stepson Hippolytus of rape after he ignores her advances. For 10 points, name this French playwright of Andromaque and Phédre.

tossups.question: This author wrote a play in an unconventional three-act form in which Hydaspes and Asaph ("high"-DASS-peez and AY-saf) are officers of King Ahasuerus (ah-hah-soo-AIR-us), who is convinced by the title character to stop a plotted massacre of Jews. That play by this man, Esther, includes extensive musical choruses, which he also used in a biblical drama in which a queen who worships Baal is killed when the high priest Joad (JOH-ad) brings Joash (JOH-ash) to power. In another play by this author, Hermione (AIR-mee-"own") vows to kill herself after Pyrrhus (PEER-us) dies, which results in Orestes going insane. This master of alexandrine verse described an illicit love affair between Aricie and Hippolyte (ar-ee-SEE and ip-oh-LEET) in a play in which the title character tells Theseus of her incestuous feelings. For 10 points, what French playwright wrote the tragedies Athalie, Andromaque, and Phedre (ah-tah-LEE, ahn-droh-MAHK, and FAY-druh)?

That play by this man, Esther, includes extensive musical choruses, which he also used in a biblical drama in which a queen who worships Baal is killed when the high priest Joad (JOH-ad) brings Joash (JOH-ash) to power. This master of alexandrine verse described an illicit love affair between Aricie and Hippolyte (ar-ee-SEE and ip-oh-LEET) in a play in which the title character tells Theseus of her incestuous feelings. For 10 points, what French playwright wrote the tragedies Athalie, Andromaque, and Phedre (ah-tah-LEE, ahn-droh-MAHK, and FAY-druh)?

tossups.question: This author deemed Classical tragedy “a school in which virtue was taught not less well than in the schools of the philosophers.” This author was known for his small vocabulary and simple diction, depicting characters “trapped” by passion. One of his characters declares “like virtue, crime has its degrees.” This playwright came out of retirement to write two Old Testament plays for the Madame de Maintenon, (*) Esther and Athalie. In Act 5 of one of his plays, Theramène describes the protagonist's horses running into the sea after being attacked by a sea monster. This playwright wrote a classic alexandrine describing a title character as “daughter of Minos and Pasiphäe.” That play introduces the character Aricie, whose love Hippolytus is incestuously loved by the title character. For 10 points, name this 17th-century French tragedian who wrote Phèdre.
tossups.question_sanitized: This author deemed Classical tragedy "a school in which virtue was taught not less well than in the schools of the philosophers." This author was known for his small vocabulary and simple diction, depicting characters "trapped" by passion. One of his characters declares "like virtue, crime has its degrees." This playwright came out of retirement to write two Old Testament plays for the Madame de Maintenon, (*) Esther and Athalie. In Act 5 of one of his plays, Theramene describes the protagonist's horses running into the sea after being attacked by a sea monster. This playwright wrote a classic alexandrine describing a title character as "daughter of Minos and Pasiphae." That play introduces the character Aricie, whose love Hippolytus is incestuously loved by the title character. For 10 points, name this 17th-century French tragedian who wrote Phedre.

tossups.question: The narrator of Proust’s In Search of Lost Time befriends Berma, an actress specializing in this man’s plays. In one of this man’s plays, a woman confides to Phenice of her love for her “all-powerful” fiance, who cannot marry her because “Rome took a hatred to the name of King.” In this man’s only comedy, Leander and Isabelle trick Chicaneau and the insane Judge Dandin into allowing their marriage. This author of Les Plaideurs (lay-play-DUR) and Bajazet wrote about Xiphares (zi-FARE-ees) and Monime (mo-NEE-may) inheriting the kingdom of (*) Mithridates (MITH-ruh-DAY-teez) in another work. He wrote about Orestes’ (aw-REST-eez) and Pyrrhus’ competition for Hermione (her-MY-oh-nee), as well as a woman’s rejection of Antiochus (an-TIE-oh-kus) and Titus (TIE-tuss). In his most famous play, Aricia (uh-REE-see-uh) is loved by Hippolytus (hip-PAUL-i-tus), the stepson and love interest of the insane title character, Theseus’ wife. For 10 points, name this seventeenth-century French tragedian of Berenice, Andromaque, and Phedre.

In this man's only comedy, Leander and Isabelle trick Chicaneau and the insane Judge Dandin into allowing their marriage. He wrote about Orestes' (aw-REST-eez) and Pyrrhus' competition for Hermione (her-MY-oh-nee), as well as a woman's rejection of Antiochus (an-TIE-oh-kus) and Titus (TIE-tuss). In his most famous play, Aricia (uh-REE-see-uh) is loved by Hippolytus (hip-PAUL-i-tus), the stepson and love interest of the insane title character, Theseus' wife. For 10 points, name this seventeenth-century French tragedian of Berenice, Andromaque, and Phedre.

tossups.question: In a play by this author, the young boy Joas is hidden in a temple to escape the murder of his siblings by the title queen so that he may survive to become king of the Jews. This author included the nobly-born servants Cleone and Cephisa in another play. This author of Athalie used a meter with a caesura in the middle of each line to write a monologue relating how a prince's horses were frightened by a bull-dragon which arose from the sea off-stage. He used that alexandrine verse to adapt a plot in which Helen’s daughter Hermione loves Pyrrhus, and another plot also derived from Euripides in which Aricie is treated like a daughter after Hippolytus is accused of raping his stepmother. For 10 points, name this 17th-century French playwright of Andromache and Phèdre.

packet.name: Chattahoochee HS + Columbia B + Toronto B

tossups.question: Near the end of one play by this author, a woman predicts that her own son will murder her next, and then try to drown his remorse for that murder by committing fresh atrocities; that play ends with a character weeping at the knees of a statue of Augustus and pledging to become a Vestal Virgin before another character's tutor is killed by a mob. This author wrote a play in which a king invokes Neptune's curse against his own son, who is killed when his chariot is attacked offstage by a sea monster. Love for Junia inspires the emperor Nero to poison the title character of his play Britannicus. The wife of Theseus accuses Hippolytus of raping her after he rejects her incestuous advances in another of his plays. For 10 points, name this French tragedian who wrote Phèdre.
tossups.question_sanitized: Near the end of one play by this author, a woman predicts that her own son will murder her next, and then try to drown his remorse for that murder by committing fresh atrocities; that play ends with a character weeping at the knees of a statue of Augustus and pledging to become a Vestal Virgin before another character's tutor is killed by a mob. This author wrote a play in which a king invokes Neptune's curse against his own son, who is killed when his chariot is attacked offstage by a sea monster. The wife of Theseus accuses Hippolytus of raping her after he rejects her incestuous advances in another of his plays. For 10 points, name this French tragedian who wrote Phedre.

tossups.question: Subligny criticized one of this author’s plays for the inexplicable knowledge of a secret passage by a supposedly foreign character. In that play, this author returned to writing “suivante” roles such as Cephise and Cleone. In another of this man’s works, the porter Petit Jean is forced to stand guard rather than sleep, and later is aided by Le Souffler, a prompter, in acting as prosecutor in the trial of the dog Citron. That work centers on Dandin, a mad judge. In his best-known work, the title character reveals her illicit love to her nurse Oenone, while the target of that love is tutored by the lecherous Theramene. The title character of that play kills herself after her husband Thesee discovers her love for Hippolyte. For 10 points, name this French playwright of Andromache and Phedre.

bonuses.leadin: A folk satire in this language titled The Stamped Paper Dance was likely composed in response to the 1675 papier timbré revolt. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A folk satire in this language titled The Stamped Paper Dance was likely composed in response to the 1675 papier timbre revolt. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this language whose ballads were surveyed by Mary-Ann Constantine. This language’s minstrel tradition inspired a namesake form of lai(“leh”) whose authors included Marie de France.

Constantine’s study centers on this Breton ballad form, often translated as “lament” or “complaint.” François-Marie Luzel published many of these ballads, which were contrasted with sonioù(“sohn-YOO”) love songs.

Olivier de Gourcuff’s preface to a Breton anthology praises Auguste Brizeux (“bree-ZEW”), who wrote a one-act comedy in verse about this author. This playwright perfected the alexandrine in tragedies like Andromaque(“ahn-droh-MAHK”) and Phèdre(“FED-ruh”).
parts_sanitized: Name this language whose ballads were surveyed by Mary-Ann Constantine. This language's minstrel tradition inspired a namesake form of lai("leh") whose authors included Marie de France.

Constantine's study centers on this Breton ballad form, often translated as "lament" or "complaint." Francois-Marie Luzel published many of these ballads, which were contrasted with soniou("sohn-YOO") love songs.

Olivier de Gourcuff's preface to a Breton anthology praises Auguste Brizeux ("bree-ZEW"), who wrote a one-act comedy in verse about this author. This playwright perfected the alexandrine in tragedies like Andromaque("ahn-droh-MAHK") and Phedre("FED-ruh").
answers_sanitized: Breton [or brezhoneg; accept Breton Ballads or Breton lais]

packet.name: F. Prelims 6 - Indiana, Minnesota, Oxford, Penn State

bonuses.leadin: Georges de Scudéry participated in the “war of pamphlets,” which inflamed a scandal involving this play and its author. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Georges de Scudery participated in the "war of pamphlets," which inflamed a scandal involving this play and its author. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play, which was accused by Cardinal Richelieu’s French Academy of violating the three unities of time, place, and action. Its title character reconciles with his love, Chimene [shee-men], after fighting off an army.
In contrast to Corneille, David G. Muller praised this other author for producing “examples of neoclassical perfection,” such as his tragedies Andromaque and Phèdre.

With Philippe Quinault and this other author, Corneille collaborated on the comedy Psyche. This author collapsed and died after finishing a performance where he played his character Argan, the title Imaginary Invalid.
parts_sanitized: Name this play, which was accused by Cardinal Richelieu's French Academy of violating the three unities of time, place, and action. Its title character reconciles with his love, Chimene [shee-men], after fighting off an army.
In contrast to Corneille, David G. Muller praised this other author for producing "examples of neoclassical perfection," such as his tragedies Andromaque and Phedre.

With Philippe Quinault and this other author, Corneille collaborated on the comedy Psyche. This author collapsed and died after finishing a performance where he played his character Argan, the title Imaginary Invalid.
answers_sanitized: Le Cid [or The Cid] (The play is by Pierre Corneille.)

packet.name: E (Cornell A, JMU A, McGill A, Columbia C, Brandeis A)

bonuses.leadin: In a play by this author, Hermione tells Cleone that she “loved” Pyrrhus “too much not to hate him,” before asking Orestes to kill him. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who also wrote a play in which a queen falls in love with Theseus’s son despite his love for the princess Aricia.
Racine’s only comedy, The Litigants, adapts this Greek author’s play The Wasps.

A Racine character with this job poisons his stepbrother and abducts his stepbrother’s fiancée. Grace Frick translated a novel in which a character with this job tells Mark about wanting to “seduce everyone and everything, even History.”
answers: Roman emperor [prompt on politician or ruler] (The Racine play is Britannicus. The novel is Memoirs of Hadrian.)

parts_sanitized: Name this author who also wrote a play in which a queen falls in love with Theseus's son despite his love for the princess Aricia.

A Racine character with this job poisons his stepbrother and abducts his stepbrother's fiancee. Grace Frick translated a novel in which a character with this job tells Mark about wanting to "seduce everyone and everything, even History."

bonuses.leadin: A performance of Corneille’s (“kor-NAY’s”) tragedy Nicomède(“nee-ko-MED”) in front of Louis XIV (“the-fourteenth”) allowed this acting troupe to make a permanent home in the Hôtel du Petit-Bourbon. For 10 points each:
parts: Name or describe this company that was founded after the Illustre Théâtre collapsed in 1645. Its most famous stars included La Grange and Madeleine Béjart (“bay-ZHAR”), and it created the genre of the comédie-ballet.
In 1664, Molière’s troupe premiered La Thébaïde(“teh-bah-EED”), the first of this author’s plays to be performed live. His other tragedies include Phèdre(“FED-ruh”).

Racine’s later quarrel with Molière came in part because he stole away this leading lady from Molière’s company. Racine wrote the title role in Andromaque(“ahn-droh-MAHK”) for this woman, while they were lovers, and Corneille wrote his Stances à Marquise(“stance ah mar-KEESS”) about his unrequited love for her.
answers: La Troupe de Molière [accept anything that says that this is the theatrical company run by Molière or Jean-Baptiste Poquelin]
answers_sanitized: La Troupe de Moliere [accept anything that says that this is the theatrical company run by Moliere or Jean-Baptiste Poquelin]
Mademoiselle Therese Du Parc [or Marquise-Therese de Gorla]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A performance of Corneille's ("kor-NAY's") tragedy Nicomede("nee-ko-MED") in front of Louis XIV ("the-fourteenth") allowed this acting troupe to make a permanent home in the Hotel du Petit-Bourbon. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name or describe this company that was founded after the Illustre Theatre collapsed in 1645. Its most famous stars included La Grange and Madeleine Bejart ("bay-ZHAR"), and it created the genre of the comedie-ballet.
In 1664, Moliere's troupe premiered La Thebaide("teh-bah-EED"), the first of this author's plays to be performed live. His other tragedies include Phedre("FED-ruh").

Racine's later quarrel with Moliere came in part because he stole away this leading lady from Moliere's company. Racine wrote the title role in Andromaque("ahn-droh-MAHK") for this woman, while they were lovers, and Corneille wrote his Stances a Marquise("stance ah mar-KEESS") about his unrequited love for her.

bonuses.leadin: In an oft-quoted speech, the title character of this play describes her mother wearing makeup to “repair the irreparable ravages of time.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play that Voltaire called the “supreme achievement of the human mind,” in which Joad and Jehoshebath agree to dethrone the title character for her worship of Baal.
Athalie was written by this author of Andromaque. The title woman poisons herself after Hippolyte’s (“ee-po-LEET’s”) death in this French tragedian’s play Phèdre (“FED-ruh”).

Racine wrote plays like Athalie in lines of this meter, which is also used for the last line of a Spenserian stanza. Named for the hero of the medieval romance that first used it, this form features two hemistichs of six syllables each separated by a caesura (“suh-ZHUR-uh”).

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about French plays with ancient Greek subjects, for 10 points each:
parts: Laurence Olivier (Oh-liv-eee-ay) was in the original cast of this author's adaptation of Sophocles' play Antigone, which criticizes Nazi occupation of France.

This contemporary of Moliere and Corneille drew inspiration from Euripides for his plays Phedre and Andromaque. Also heavily inspired by the Bible, he wrote a play about the wife of Judah who worships the god Baal in his play, Athalie, as well as a play about Esther.
A notable difference between Euripides' Hippolytus and Racine's Phedre is the latter's inclusion of this character, Hippolytus' love interest.

bonuses.leadin: Raymond Picard, a scholar of this author, wrote the article “New Criticism or New Fraud?” in response to a book on him by Roland Barthes [roh-LAWN BART]. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 17th-century French playwright. This author of the tragedies Athalie [ah-tah-LEE] and Berenice wrote the title role of his play, Andromaque, for actress Therese du Parc.
Racine’s Phèdre was based on this ancient Greek writer’s play Hippolytus. His other tragedies include Medea and The Bacchae.

This Frenchman argued that actors should distance themselves from roles, instead of focusing on emotion, in his essay “Paradox of Acting.” He wrote a philosophical work about the “dream” of mathematician Jean d’Alembert [dall-ahm-BARE].

bonuses.leadin: Name the following about 17th century French drama, for 10 points each.
parts: One of the few comedians, this playwright of The School for Wives and The Hypochondriac wrote about the alcoholic woodcutter Sganarelle in The Doctor Despite Himself.

This other dramatist was known for his adaptations of ancient or Classical works; he re-told the story of the Trojan War in Andromaque, while Voltaire called his Athalie the greatest triumph of the human mind.

In this play by Pierre Corneille, the titular martyr’s wife Pauline unsuccessfully tries to convince him to renounce his Christianity. The protagonist entrusts her to the Roman soldier Servetus before he is put to death.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Name the following about 17th century French drama, for 10 points each.
parts_sanitized: One of the few comedians, this playwright of The School for Wives and The Hypochondriac wrote about the alcoholic woodcutter Sganarelle in The Doctor Despite Himself.

This other dramatist was known for his adaptations of ancient or Classical works; he re-told the story of the Trojan War in Andromaque, while Voltaire called his Athalie the greatest triumph of the human mind.

In this play by Pierre Corneille, the titular martyr's wife Pauline unsuccessfully tries to convince him to renounce his Christianity. The protagonist entrusts her to the Roman soldier Servetus before he is put to death.

bonuses.leadin: Jonathan Swift’s The Battle of the Books was a satire on this debate. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this debate, originating in France, that hinged on whether literature should follow classical rules such as the Unities, or more generally whether the best literature was old or new.
This playwright generally sided with the Ancients in the quarrel. The restoration of Joash leads the kingdom of Judah to re-adopt Judaism in his play Athalie, and he also wrote Phedre.
answers_sanitized: Quarrel between the Ancients and the Moderns
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Jonathan Swift's The Battle of the Books was a satire on this debate. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this debate, originating in France, that hinged on whether literature should follow classical rules such as the Unities, or more generally whether the best literature was old or new.
This playwright generally sided with the Ancients in the quarrel. The restoration of Joash leads the kingdom of Judah to re-adopt Judaism in his play Athalie, and he also wrote Phedre.

bonuses.leadin: A character in this play mockingly asks another “Can you love?” after he admits that his love for Aricia is the main reason why he intends to leave Troezen. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play in which the title character poisons herself after her nurse Oenone hatches a plot to convince Theseus that she was seduced by his son Hippolytus, leading to his exile and subsequent death.
Phédre was written by this classical French tragedian who described the title character’s marriage to Pyrrhus in order to protect her son Astyanax in Andromaque.
Racine was a contemporary of this French author of the Horace-inspired L’Art poétique, whose translation of Longinus re-instated “the sublime” as an aesthetic category in the 17th-century.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A character in this play mockingly asks another "Can you love?" after he admits that his love for Aricia is the main reason why he intends to leave Troezen. For 10 points each:

Phedre was written by this classical French tragedian who described the title character's marriage to Pyrrhus in order to protect her son Astyanax in Andromaque.
Racine was a contemporary of this French author of the Horace-inspired L'Art poetique, whose translation of Longinus re-instated "the sublime" as an aesthetic category in the 17th-century.

bonuses.leadin: In this play, Nero abducts a young woman who is betrothed to the title character. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this tragedy which concludes with Junia promising to become a vestal virgin and an angry mob killing Narcisse.
This other work by the author of Britannicus sees Thésée abandon the title character for six months, only for that woman to develop a passion for Theseus’ son Hippolyte.
Both Britannicus and Phedre were penned by this 17th Century French playwright known for his tragedies based on Classical subjects.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In this play, Nero abducts a young woman who is betrothed to the title character. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this tragedy which concludes with Junia promising to become a vestal virgin and an angry mob killing Narcisse.
This other work by the author of Britannicus sees Thesee abandon the title character for six months, only for that woman to develop a passion for Theseus' son Hippolyte.
Both Britannicus and Phedre were penned by this 17th Century French playwright known for his tragedies based on Classical subjects.

bonuses.leadin: This play centers on Nero’s sudden obsession with the title character's fiancée, Junia. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play, in which Nero pretends to his mother, Agrippina, that he will relent in his pursuit, but, in actuality, has the title character killed off stage.
Britannicus is a work of this 17th-century French playwright of Andromaque and Athalie.

In this other play by Jean Racine, the wife of Theseus is the center of much drama concerning her love for her stepson Hippolytus, who is himself in love with the Aricie, a member of the bloodline of Athens' royalty.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This play centers on Nero's sudden obsession with the title character's fiancee, Junia. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this play, in which Nero pretends to his mother, Agrippina, that he will relent in his pursuit, but, in actuality, has the title character killed off stage.
Britannicus is a work of this 17th-century French playwright of Andromaque and Athalie.

In this other play by Jean Racine, the wife of Theseus is the center of much drama concerning her love for her stepson Hippolytus, who is himself in love with the Aricie, a member of the bloodline of Athens' royalty.

bonuses.leadin: This poet claimed “wine exalts the will, hashish destroys it” in his de Quincey-inspired book on recreational drug use, Artificial Paradises. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this dissolute French author who addressed the “hypocrite reader” in the prefatory poem of a book that also includes the sections “Parisian Scenes” and “Spleen and Ideal,” Les Fleurs du Mal.
Another Baudelaire poem describes how the “opulent display” of these title objects make his otherwise-naked love look “like Moorish concubines.” A Diderot novel is titled after some indiscreet ones.
“Les Bijoux” was written in the alexandrine meter, like the plays of this author of Bajazet and Andromaque.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poet claimed "wine exalts the will, hashish destroys it" in his de Quincey-inspired book on recreational drug use, Artificial Paradises. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this dissolute French author who addressed the "hypocrite reader" in the prefatory poem of a book that also includes the sections "Parisian Scenes" and "Spleen and Ideal," Les Fleurs du Mal.
Another Baudelaire poem describes how the "opulent display" of these title objects make his otherwise-naked love look "like Moorish concubines." A Diderot novel is titled after some indiscreet ones.
"Les Bijoux" was written in the alexandrine meter, like the plays of this author of Bajazet and Andromaque.

bonuses.leadin: This man only wrote one comedy, Les Plaideurs. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this French dramatist best known for a tragedy in which Theseus’ curse leads to the death of Hippolytus, Phèdre.
In this play by Racine, the high priest Joad attempts to install Joash on the throne to replace the title character, who has abandoned Judaism to worship Baal.

Racine’s plays very closely adhere to this set of three classical rules of drama, introduced in Aristotle’s Poetics. They state that a play should have a single main plot, and take place in a single location over no more than 24 hours.
answers_sanitized: dramatic unities [or classical unities; or Aristotelian unities]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This man only wrote one comedy, Les Plaideurs. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this French dramatist best known for a tragedy in which Theseus' curse leads to the death of Hippolytus, Phedre.
In this play by Racine, the high priest Joad attempts to install Joash on the throne to replace the title character, who has abandoned Judaism to worship Baal.

Racine's plays very closely adhere to this set of three classical rules of drama, introduced in Aristotle's Poetics. They state that a play should have a single main plot, and take place in a single location over no more than 24 hours.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, name the following French dramatists.
parts: This comedian produced Tartuffe and The Imaginary Invalid; he died during a production of that latter play.

This writer wrote a trilogy that includes Begearss, a scheming Irishman that tries to fleece others, like Count Almaviva. That trilogy consists of The Guilty Mother, The Marriage of Figaro and The Barber of Seville.

This author converted to Jansenism and spent the last years of his life writing the Spiritual Canticles. His plays include one about a sadistic Nero, who poisons the title character and drives off Junie into a convent.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: For 10 points each, name the following French dramatists.
parts_sanitized: This comedian produced Tartuffe and The Imaginary Invalid; he died during a production of that latter play.

This writer wrote a trilogy that includes Begearss, a scheming Irishman that tries to fleece others, like Count Almaviva. That trilogy consists of The Guilty Mother, The Marriage of Figaro and The Barber of Seville.

This author converted to Jansenism and spent the last years of his life writing the Spiritual Canticles. His plays include one about a sadistic Nero, who poisons the title character and drives off Junie into a convent.

bonuses.leadin: One of this author’s letters describes how the chef Vatel killed himself after mistakenly thinking an order of fish was delayed. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this woman, whose letters to her daughter Francoise are one of the best sources about French court life in the 17th century. She was a good friend of Nicholas Fouquet, and wrote many letters about his arrest and trial.

Madame de Sevigne championed Corneille over this author, although she reported enjoying his play Esther. This playwright may be better known for writing neoclassical tragedies such as Andromaque and Phedre.

Madame de Sevigne was also good friends with Madame de Lafayette, who wrote this book often considered the first French novel. This historical novel is set in the 16th century, and focuses on the title character’s forbidden love for the Duke of Nemours.
answers: The Princess of Cleves [or La Princesse de Cleves]
answers_sanitized: Madame de Sevigne [or Marie de Rabutin-Chantal]
The Princess of Cleves [or La Princesse de Cleves]
